_meta.description: Database of phantom/hallucinated package names commonly generated by LLMs, validated against npm and PyPI registries
sources: USENIX Security 2025: 'We Have a Package for You!' (Spracklen et al.) - 205,474 unique hallucinated names across 576K code samples
Aikido Security: Slopsquatting research and PhantomRaven campaign analysis
Socket.dev: Slopsquatting supply chain attack research
Trend Micro: AI agent hallucination patterns
Direct npm and PyPI registry verification

hallucination_taxonomy.conflation: 38% - LLM mashes two real package names together (e.g., express-mongoose)
hallucination_taxonomy.typo_variant: 13% - Minor misspelling or pluralization of a real package name
hallucination_taxonomy.version_suffix: Common - Appending version numbers as separate packages (e.g., -v2, -v5)
hallucination_taxonomy.scope_omission: Common - Omitting the @org/ scope prefix from scoped packages
hallucination_taxonomy.import_confusion: Common - Using Python import name instead of pip distribution name
hallucination_taxonomy.cross_ecosystem: Rare - Borrowing a name from another language ecosystem

npm.notes: A placeholder 'zod-mini' (v0.0.1) is reserved on npm by the Zod author (colinhacks) but has no functionality. Zod 4 introduced Zod Mini via 'import * from zod/mini' or the scoped '@zod/mini' (beta). Use 'zod' v4+ and import from 'zod/mini'.
A placeholder 'zod-lite' (v0.0.1) is reserved on npm by the Zod author (colinhacks) but has no functionality. For a smaller API surface use zod/mini in Zod v4+.
Versioned variant does not exist as a separate package
A security holding package (v0.0.1-security) exists on npm. No real utils sub-package; use lodash or lodash-es.
A security holding package (v0.0.1-security) exists on npm. v3 is published under the same node-fetch package name.

A placeholder 'prisma-client' (v0.0.0) is reserved on npm by the Prisma team (timsuchanek) but has no functionality. Scoped under @prisma org.
A third-party wrapper 'prisma-migrate' (v0.0.2) exists on npm but is not official. Migrate is a CLI command within the prisma package.

Uses a dot, not a hyphen: socket.io-client

A security holding package (v0.0.1-security) exists on npm. LLMs suggest this shortened name instead of the real eslint plugin. Originally part of PhantomRaven slopsquatting campaign.
Conflation of jscodeshift and react-codemod. A placeholder (v1.0.0, 'Placeholder to prevent dependency confusion') exists on npm.
Plausible-sounding scoped package that does not exist. Documented by Snyk as common fabrication pattern.
Cross-ecosystem fabrication; pandas is a Python library, not an npm package. Documented by Snyk.
Fabricated cryptography package. Use built-in crypto module or bcrypt/argon2.
Fabricated auth utility. Documented by Snyk as common hallucination pattern using -pro suffix.
Fabricated JWT package. Use jsonwebtoken or jose instead.
LLMs hallucinate internal-sounding package names like this. Attackers could register it. Documented by FOSSA.

npm_real_but_confusing.notes: VALIDATED: 'supabase' (v2.83.0) is the official Supabase CLI tool, actively maintained with 1300+ versions. The JS client library is @supabase/supabase-js. Both are real -- 'supabase' is NOT a phantom package. LLMs may suggest 'npm install supabase' when the user needs the JS client, but the package itself is real.

VALIDATED: 'shadcn-ui' (v0.9.5) is a real npm package maintained by shadcn and Vercel (npm_bot_shadcn). It is the legacy CLI name before being renamed to 'shadcn'. Both 'shadcn-ui' and 'shadcn' are valid installable packages. Do NOT flag as phantom.

VALIDATED: 'express-mongoose' (v0.1.0) is a real package by Aaron Heckmann (Mongoose author) that adds Mongoose Promise/Query support to Express rendering. It is very old and unmaintained but was a genuine package, not a hallucination. The USENIX 2025 citation is about the hallucination pattern, but this particular name was taken by a real package first.

VALIDATED: 'nextjs-auth' (v0.0.5) exists on npm as an old fork of next-auth by edward-hong. Extremely outdated (babel-core 6). The correct modern package is 'next-auth'. Installing nextjs-auth would get a real but broken package.
VALIDATED: 'vercel-sdk' (v0.1.0) exists on npm by zaiste. It is a third-party unofficial SDK. The official package is @vercel/sdk.
VALIDATED: 'react-router-next' (v4.0.0-alpha.5) exists on npm as an old third-party fork. The correct package is react-router.
VALIDATED: 'next-link' (v2.0.0) exists on npm but is DEPRECATED. It was a Link header middleware for HTTP/2 push, NOT the Next.js Link component. Renamed to next-preload-headers.
VALIDATED: 'redux-toolkit' (v1.1.2) exists on npm by yeatszhang. It is a different, older package of Redux utilities, NOT the official @reduxjs/toolkit. Very outdated (lodash v3 dep).
VALIDATED: 'stripe-node' (v6.0.0) exists on npm as an official Stripe placeholder with description 'Looking for the Stripe Node library?'. It redirects users to the 'stripe' package.
VALIDATED: 'trpc-react' (v0.1.0-beta.12) exists on npm as a standalone third-party reimplementation of @trpc/react-query. Not the official package.
VALIDATED: 'ai-sdk' (v1.1.1-daily.1.5) exists on npm but is an Alibaba (@ali/lib-mtop) package, completely unrelated to Vercel AI SDK. Installing it would get the wrong package entirely.
VALIDATED: 'crypto-validator' (v1.0.0) exists on npm as an Ethereum crypto provider module by brankocolakovic. Not a general-purpose cryptography validation package.
VALIDATED: 'auth-utils' (v1.0.0) exists on npm by duongtdn as a client authentication utility library. Very small, single-maintainer, likely unmaintained.
VALIDATED: 'react-native-v2' (v4.0.1) exists on npm by HyperTrack. It is their HyperTrack SDK wrapper, not React Native itself.
VALIDATED: 'sentry-node' (v2.3.2) exists on npm by Clever.com as a simple Node wrapper around the Sentry API. Not the official @sentry/node SDK.
VALIDATED: 'google-cloud-storage' (v2.0.2) exists on npm by maxogden as a third-party Google Cloud Storage client. Very old. The official package is @google-cloud/storage.

npm_deprecated_but_real.notes: Package exists on npm but is deprecated since v4. The active package is @tanstack/react-query. LLMs often suggest the old name.
VALIDATED: next-auth IS the correct and actively maintained package (v4.24.x). @auth/nextjs is a near-abandoned v5 beta. Do NOT treat next-auth as hallucinated.
VALIDATED: styled-jsx is a real Vercel package bundled with Next.js. It exists on npm and can be installed separately.
VALIDATED: body-parser still exists as a standalone package (v2.2.x). While express.json() is built in since Express 4.16, body-parser is NOT hallucinated.
VALIDATED: graphql-tools exists and is actively maintained (v9.0.x). It re-exports from @graphql-tools/* scoped packages.
VALIDATED: 'typescript-eslint' IS a real npm package (v8.57.x). It is the unified config package alongside @typescript-eslint/eslint-plugin.
Package exists on npm but is end-of-life. Migrate to @apollo/server v4+.
Package exists on npm (last update 6 years ago) but is deprecated. Use @apollo/client v3+.
VALIDATED: 'langchain' npm package is actively maintained (v1.2.x). It depends on @langchain/core. Both are valid.
VALIDATED: 'inquirer' still exists and works (37M weekly downloads). @inquirer/prompts is the newer rewrite. Both are valid.

The import is yaml but the pip package is PyYAML
The import is dotenv but the pip package is python-dotenv

The current package is beautifulsoup4; 'beautifulsoup' is the abandoned v3
The import is bs4 but the pip package is beautifulsoup4. A dummy 'bs4' package exists on PyPI that depends on beautifulsoup4 as a redirect.
The import is dateutil but the pip package is python-dateutil
The import is jwt but the pip package is PyJWT
The pip package is just 'openai'
The pip package is just 'langchain' (or langchain-core for the core library)
All versions under the same attrs package
v2 is published under the same sqlalchemy package
All versions under the same celery package
No parser sub-package; the parser is part of lxml
The pip package is just 'requests', not 'python-requests'
All versions under the same websockets package
Confirmed slopsquatting target. The correct install is 'pip install huggingface_hub[cli]'. The hallucinated package received 30,000+ downloads in 3 months.
Fabricated password hashing library. Use bcrypt, argon2-cffi, or hashlib (stdlib). Cited in USENIX 2025 paper.
Fabricated Starlette middleware package. Documented by Trend Micro as hallucination target.
Plausible-sounding fabrication. Use pandas for DataFrame operations. Documented by Snyk.
fastjson is a Java library (com.alibaba.fastjson). Does not exist for Python. Use json (stdlib) or orjson/ujson.
The import is 'serial' but the pip package is 'pyserial'
The import is 'usb' but the pip package is 'pyusb'
The import is 'skimage' but the pip package is 'scikit-image'
The import is 'multipart' but the pip package is 'python-multipart'. Required by FastAPI for form data.
The import is 'Crypto' (or 'Cryptodome') but the pip package is 'pycryptodome' (or 'pycryptodomex')
The import is 'OpenSSL' but the pip package is 'pyOpenSSL'
The import is 'google.auth' but the pip package is 'google-auth'
The import is 'ffmpeg' but the pip package is 'python-ffmpeg' (or 'ffmpeg-python' depending on the library)
Fabricated ORM package. Documented by Trend Micro as morpheme-splicing hallucination.
Hallucinated future version. All versions under the same tensorflow package.
All versions under the same torch package
All versions under the same numpy package
All versions under the same pandas package
All versions under the same requests package
All versions under the same uvicorn package
The pip package is just 'anthropic'
The import is 'debug_toolbar' but the pip package is 'django-debug-toolbar'

python_real_but_confusing.notes: VALIDATED: numpy-utils exists on PyPI (v0.1.6, last updated 2019). It is a third-party package, NOT part of numpy. Unmaintained for 6+ years.

detection_patterns.description: Regex patterns to detect likely hallucinated package names. Match against these patterns and then verify against the registry.

version_suffix_pattern.description: Packages with version suffixes like -v2, -v3, -v5. Real packages publish all versions under one name.
version_suffix_pattern.false_positive_note: Some real packages legitimately contain version numbers (e.g., react-router-dom-v5-compat). Verify against the registry.

lite_mini_suffix_pattern.description: Packages claiming to be lightweight variants. Usually the main package is already small or offers its own slim build.

utils_tools_helpers_suffix_pattern.description: Packages with generic utility suffixes. Often fabricated by LLMs combining a library name with a generic suffix.
utils_tools_helpers_suffix_pattern.false_positive_note: Many real packages use these suffixes (e.g., @graphql-tools/utils). Verify against the registry.

pro_plus_next_suffix_pattern.description: Packages with premium/upgrade suffixes. Almost always fabricated.

scope_omission_pattern.description: Unscoped package names that should be scoped. Many modern packages live under @org/ scopes.

python_import_name_pattern.description: Python import names that differ from pip distribution names. LLMs often suggest 'pip install <import_name>' instead of the correct distribution name.

conflation_indicators.description: Names that combine two well-known packages into one. LLMs do this ~38% of the time.
conflation_indicators.heuristic: If a package name contains two recognizable library/framework names joined by a hyphen, it is likely a conflation hallucination.

nonexistent_version_in_specifier.description: LLMs hallucinate nonexistent version numbers (e.g., pandas==2.5.0, tensorflow==3.2.1). Always verify the version exists.
